question: What is the purpose of the pre-flight briefing?
options: To check meal availability
To encourage communication, build rapport, and promote team performance
To discuss passenger complaints from previous flights
To finalize ticketing and boarding information
correct_text: To encourage communication, build rapport, and promote team performance

question: When must the entire crew meet for the pre-flight briefing?
options: At any time during boarding
Only after passengers are seated
On time, prior to the first flight of the day, in the location planned by the Commander
On time, prior to the first flight of the day, in the location written on CIF
correct_text: On time, prior to the first flight of the day, in the location planned by the Commander

question: In which locations may the pre-flight briefing be conducted?
options: Only in the aircraft cockpit
Only at the hotel
Briefing room, aircraft cabin with no passengers, hotel, or private area such as a screened gate area
Passenger seating area during boarding
correct_text: Briefing room, aircraft cabin with no passengers, hotel, or private area such as a screened gate area

question: If one or more crew members are not native speakers, the briefing must be conducted in:
options: The Commander’s native language
The language of the airline’s headquarters
English
Any language chosen by the majority of the crew
correct_text: English

question: When flight crew and cabin crew are on different schedules or transit passengers remain on board, the Commander should:
options: Cancel the pre-flight briefing
Brief all crew separately
Brief the SCCM/CCM1, who will then brief the cabin crew
Skip the briefing to save time
correct_text: Brief the SCCM/CCM1, who will then brief the cabin crew

question: The Commander’s briefing should:
options: Be as long as possible, covering every technical detail
Only discuss meal service and crew breaks
Be short and cover key operational and safety topics
Be optional for experienced crews
correct_text: Be short and cover key operational and safety topics

question: Which of the following is NOT typically covered in the Commander’s briefing?
options: Weather conditions and expected turbulence
Aircraft type and version
Special passenger categories
Catering supplier contract details
correct_text: Catering supplier contract details

question: Which of these topics may be dedicated specifically to the SCCM?
options: Weather report
Flight times
Verification that all personal documents are valid
Aircraft type
correct_text: Verification that all personal documents are valid

question: Which combination best describes the minimum required content of the SCCM briefing?
options: Allocation of meal service duties, number of passengers, boarding times
Aircraft registration, gate number, boarding group assignments
Crew station allocation & emergency duties, aircraft-specific procedures, equipment, area/type of operation, special passenger categories, and safety knowledge review
Weather conditions, flight time, altitude
correct_text: Crew station allocation & emergency duties, aircraft-specific procedures, equipment, area/type of operation, special passenger categories, and safety knowledge review

question: During the SCCM briefing, emphasis must be placed on which group of passengers to ensure cabin crew readiness?
options: Unaccompanied minors, premium passengers, and passengers with pets
VIP passengers, frequent flyers, and tour groups
Passengers with reduced mobility, disabilities, infants, and stretcher cases
Passengers seated in overwing exits only
correct_text: Passengers with reduced mobility, disabilities, infants, and stretcher cases

question: Three random Safety & Emergency questions from the CCOM must be discussed to:
options: Verify who has studied the manual recently
Evaluate crew morale before flight
Refresh and validate operational knowledge and emergency preparedness
Record results for performance appraisals
correct_text: Refresh and validate operational knowledge and emergency preparedness

question: If the SCCM becomes incapacitated mid-flight, who should assume their duties according to pre-flight planning?
options: The youngest crew member
The Commander
The second most experienced CC identified during briefing
Any available ground staff member
correct_text: The second most experienced CC identified during briefing

question: Which of the following would be a critical error in SCCM pre-flight briefing?
options: Failing to ask safety & emergency questions
Not assigning stations before boarding
Both A and B
Conducting the briefing in English when everyone is a native speaker
correct_text: Both A and B

question: According to procedures, if a cabin crew member discovers a missing or unserviceable item of emergency equipment, they must:
options: Record it for post-flight reporting
Replace it themselves if possible
Report it to the Commander immediately
Continue operations without reporting to avoid delays
correct_text: Report it to the Commander immediately

question: Under what condition may additional uplift of equipment cause a flight delay?
options: If it is convenient for the ground crew
If the cabin crew requests it
It must always cause a delay until completed
Only if the Commander accepts the delay
correct_text: Only if the Commander accepts the delay

question: When must cabin crew perform the emergency equipment check?
options: Only once per day, regardless of aircraft changes
Only if the Commander asks for it
Immediately after boarding, prior to flight, and again after crew changes or returning to an unattended aircraft
Only before the first flight of the duty period
correct_text: Immediately after boarding, prior to flight, and again after crew changes or returning to an unattended aircraft

question: Which of the following best describes what CCMs must ensure about emergency equipment?
options: It is present and unsealed for immediate use
It is visible to passengers at all times
It is stored in the galley compartments only
It is present, correctly stowed, accessible, serviceable, secured, and sealed if applicable
correct_text: It is present, correctly stowed, accessible, serviceable, secured, and sealed if applicable

question: If the emergency equipment is located in an overhead bin, what is strictly prohibited?
options: Placing company manuals near it
Storing crew meals above it
Covering or blocking it with baggage, coats, or other items
All mentioned
correct_text: All mentioned

question: When must a pre-flight security search be performed?
options: Only when passengers report something suspicious
Once per aircraft each week
Before the first flight of the day for the crew or other cases specified in OM-A, when all ground staff have left the aircraft and before passenger embarkation
Only after catering is loaded
correct_text: Before the first flight of the day for the crew or other cases specified in OM-A, when all ground staff have left the aircraft and before passenger embarkation

question: Which best describes what searchers are looking for during a pre-flight security search?
options: Loose items belonging to crew
Misplaced catering supplies
Anything that should not be there, cannot be readily identified, is out of place, or appears concealed
Signs of missing passenger property
correct_text: Anything that should not be there, cannot be readily identified, is out of place, or appears concealed

question: What areas must be included in a pre-flight security search?
options: Only cabin seats and overhead bins
Passenger cabin and flight deck
Passenger cabin only if there are international passengers
Passenger cabin, lavatories, galleys, and cargo compartments accessible from cabin
correct_text: Passenger cabin, lavatories, galleys, and cargo compartments accessible from cabin

question: If a suspicious object is found and Commander/Co-pilot are not present, what is the correct action?
options: Wait until flight crew returns
Move the item to the galley and continue search
Contact Airport Security immediately
Hide the item until flight crew arrives
correct_text: Contact Airport Security immediately

question: If a passenger leaves the aircraft before departure, what is the recommended action?
options: Ignore it, as bags will be removed automatically
Only check that passenger’s seat
Ask nearby passengers to visually confirm their own belongings and remove any unattributed items
Conduct a full cabin security search again
correct_text: Ask nearby passengers to visually confirm their own belongings and remove any unattributed items

question: Which of the following must be checked during lavatory inspection before departure?
options: Only toilet paper and soap
Only lights and flush system
Waste bin flap, flush function, water availability, lights, call button function, smoke detector, cleanliness, and supplies
Only that the lavatory door locks
correct_text: Waste bin flap, flush function, water availability, lights, call button function, smoke detector, cleanliness, and supplies

question: What does the cabin crew need to confirm about the lavatory call button?
options: It is easy to reach for children
Amber light illuminates on lav wall and is displayed on AIP, AAP, and ACP
It is switched off during taxi
It is functioning only for use by crew
correct_text: Amber light illuminates on lav wall and is displayed on AIP, AAP, and ACP

question: If foreign objects are found inserted into or blocking a smoke detector, cabin crew must:
options: Remove them quietly and continue
Ask passengers nearby if they know who did it
Inform the Commander immediately
Log it after flight completion
correct_text: Inform the Commander immediately

question: Which of the following is NOT a part of the lavatory pre-flight check?
options: Toilet paper supply
Liquid soap
Checking galley waste disposal
Smoke detector functionality
correct_text: Checking galley waste disposal

question: Cabin crew stations vary according to:
options: Time of day
Passenger load
Aircraft type
Cabin service schedule
correct_text: Aircraft type

question: Each CCM seat is fitted with:
options: Lap belt only
Shoulder harness only
Combination seat belt/shoulder harness
Standard passenger seatbelt
correct_text: Combination seat belt/shoulder harness

question: During taxi, the Commander instructs all CCMs to strap in. Which of the following is the MOST correct action?
options: Sit in the nearest passenger seat and fasten the seatbelt only
Return to assigned station and fasten lap strap loosely
Occupy assigned jump seat, fasten lap strap and harness securely, and remain seated
Stay standing near the galley to monitor passengers
correct_text: Occupy assigned jump seat, fasten lap strap and harness securely, and remain seated

question: While performing pre-flight checks, you find the interphone at your station is not audible when tested. What is the correct action?
options: Make a note and report it after landing
Continue the flight since PA system is working
Report the fault immediately to the SCCM/Commander so it can be rectified before departure
Use another station’s interphone during emergencies
correct_text: Report the fault immediately to the SCCM/Commander so it can be rectified before departure

question: Why must harnesses be left open when not in use during flight?
options: To reduce wear on the belts
To allow quick evacuation if needed
To enable rapid donning in case of turbulence or sudden need to strap in
To make the cabin look tidy for passengers
correct_text: To enable rapid donning in case of turbulence or sudden need to strap in

question: During your pre-flight check, you find several seat belts hanging into the aisle. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Leave them as they are since passengers will fasten them
Report to the SCCM and continue checks
Place them on the seat, ensuring they are ready for use and not obstructing the aisle
Wait until boarding starts and ask passengers to fix them
correct_text: Place them on the seat, ensuring they are ready for use and not obstructing the aisle

question: Why must window blinds be open and curtains secured prior to takeoff?
options: To allow natural light in for passenger comfort
To make the cabin look brighter and welcoming
To allow crew and passengers to observe outside conditions during critical phases
To save time for cleaning crew
correct_text: To allow crew and passengers to observe outside conditions during critical phases

question: Which of the following findings would require you to report an issue rather than just correcting it yourself?
options: A seat belt lying across the seat
A carpet section that is loose and poses a tripping hazard
A tray table left open
A window blind closed
correct_text: A carpet section that is loose and poses a tripping hazard

question: If a seat pocket is missing its safety card during the pre-flight check, what is the correct procedure?
options: Replace it after boarding is complete
Ask a passenger to share one from another row
Notify SCCM/Commander so a replacement card can be placed before passengers board
Leave it empty since safety demonstration will cover procedures
correct_text: Notify SCCM/Commander so a replacement card can be placed before passengers board

question: During a pre-flight galley check, you notice a waste bin flap that does not close completely. What is the correct action?
options: Ignore it since bin can still be used
Tape it shut temporarily
Report it so it can be repaired or replaced before departure
Leave a note for next crew
correct_text: Report it so it can be repaired or replaced before departure

question: If a galley trolley’s braking device is inoperative, what must you do?
options: Use the trolley carefully and monitor during flight
Replace the trolley and label it “INOPERATIVE”
Secure it with a seatbelt in the galley
Leave it for maintenance after the flight
correct_text: Replace the trolley and label it “INOPERATIVE”

question: Why must all stowages be checked to be lockable and securable?
options: To prevent catering crew from accessing them
To prevent items from falling out during turbulence or emergency
To ensure quick loading of catering boxes
correct_text: To prevent items from falling out during turbulence or emergency

question: During your check, you find an oven with heavy oil residue and burnt food parts. What is the best action?
options: Clean it after takeoff
Ignore it since ovens are rarely inspected
Report it to SCCM for entry in the Cabin Log Report for cleaning
Mark it with a sticker but continue using it
correct_text: Report it to SCCM for entry in the Cabin Log Report for cleaning

question: When testing a trolley’s braking system on board, what is the correct procedure?
options: Check brakes only after boarding
Apply brakes, load trolley fully, and push with maximum force
Press the red brake pedal, push and pull the trolley with normal operational force, and verify wheels stop
D) Roll the trolley down the aisle to see if it moves
correct_text: Press the red brake pedal, push and pull the trolley with normal operational force, and verify wheels stop

question: If a trolley is fully loaded but moves freely when brake pedal is pressed and normal operational force is applied, what does this indicate?
options: The trolley is ready for service
The trolley should be used with caution
The braking system needs to be adjusted
The trolley is too light
correct_text: The braking system needs to be adjusted

question: What must cabin crew do if a defective braking system is discovered before or during flight?
options: Use the trolley for light service only
Stow and secure the trolley, label it “INOPERATIVE,” report to SCCM/Commander, and record in Cabin Log
Leave it in the galley for maintenance after landing
correct_text: Stow and secure the trolley, label it “INOPERATIVE,” report to SCCM/Commander, and record in Cabin Log

question: Why must carts and trolleys always be secured during taxi, take-off, and landing?
options: To prevent them from rolling and injuring passengers or crew
To reduce noise during take-off
To save space in the galley
To comply with catering company policies
correct_text: To prevent them from rolling and injuring passengers or crew

question: When a trolley is taken out of its stowage for service, which of the following is mandatory?
options: It must be placed sideways to block the aisle
It must be secured with passenger seatbelts
It must always be locked and attended unless brakes are engaged
correct_text: It must always be locked and attended unless brakes are engaged

question: When must the interphone system check be performed?
options: Only after boarding is complete
During the emergency equipment check prior to flight
Only if a defect is suspected
correct_text: During the emergency equipment check prior to flight

question: What must cabin crew obtain before performing interphone checks between cabin and flight deck?
options: Permission from flight crew
Permission from SCCM
No permission needed
correct_text: Permission from flight crew

question: What is the correct call-out for a normal cabin-to-flight deck interphone check?
options: Caller: “Cabin Call” / Receiver: “Cabin Call received”
Caller: “System Check” / Receiver: “System OK”
Caller: “Interphone Check” / Receiver: “Interphone checked
Caller: “Radio Check” / Receiver: “Radio clear”
correct_text: Caller: “Interphone Check” / Receiver: “Interphone checked

question: During an emergency call check cabin-to-cabin, the correct response is:
options: Caller: “Emergency Check” / Receiver: “Check Complete”
Caller: “Cabin Ready” / Receiver: “Cabin Ready”
Caller: “Emergency Call Check” / Receiver: “Emergency Call Checked
Caller: “Alert Call” / Receiver: “Alert Call Received”
correct_text: Caller: “Emergency Call Check” / Receiver: “Emergency Call Checked

question: When performing a PA check, the correct callout is:
options: “PA Test''
“Cabin Ready”
“PA Check”
“Test Complete”
correct_text: “PA Check”

question: The Cabin Log Report (CLR) should be used for:
options: Only MEL items and safety-related defects
Non-safety-related cabin equipment affecting comfort or convenience
Safety-related cabin equipment affecting comfort or convenience
correct_text: Safety-related cabin equipment affecting comfort or convenience

question: If a cabin defect might be safety-related but you are unsure, what is the correct procedure?
options: Write it in the CLR anyway
Wait until the end of the flight to decide
Consult the Commander for clarification
Leave it unreported until maintenance finds it
correct_text: Consult the Commander for clarification

question: If a safety-related item must be logged in the CLR, what is required?
options: SCCM writes it without informing anyone
Technical staff approval first
Commander’s permission before writing it in
correct_text: Commander’s permission before writing it in

question: When is the Cabin Log Report removed from the aircraft?
options: After every flight
Whenever there is a major defect
Only when the last page is completely used, and only by authorized technical personne
At the request of cabin crew
correct_text: Only when the last page is completely used, and only by authorized technical personne

question: Why are cabin lights dimmed during take-off and landing at night or low light conditions?
options: To save electrical power
To create a relaxing atmosphere
To allow passengers’ and crew’s eyesight to adjust to darkness
To avoid disturbing sleeping passengers
correct_text: To allow passengers’ and crew’s eyesight to adjust to darkness

question: What must be done before dimming cabin lights for take-off or landing?
options: Turn on all lavatory lights
Close all window blinds
Make the appropriate passenger announcement
Turn off reading lights
correct_text: Make the appropriate passenger announcement

question: Which of the following is NOT permitted during normal operations?
options: Dimming cabin lights at night for passenger comfort
Keeping entrance areas well lit during embarkation
Keeping the cabin in complete darkness
Using passenger reading lights during take-off
correct_text: Keeping the cabin in complete darkness

question: Safety and emergency demonstration announcements must always be given:
options: In complete darkness for passenger attention
After take-off only
In good lighting so passengers can clearly see procedures
With reading lights off
correct_text: In good lighting so passengers can clearly see procedures

question: Passenger reading lights can be:
options: Used only during cruise
Used only in business class
Switched on for take-off and landing during dark periods
Disabled during night operations
correct_text: Switched on for take-off and landing during dark periods

question: Who must grant permission before any person other than the operating crew can enter the flight deck?
options: The Dispatcher
The Commander
The First Officer
The SCCM
correct_text: The Commander

question: When should employees or passive crew visit the flight deck whenever possible?
options: During take-off
During in-flight service
Before boarding or after disembarkation of passengers
Only during cruise
correct_text: Before boarding or after disembarkation of passengers

question: If a person needs to visit the flight deck while passengers are on board, cabin crew must:
options: Direct the person to go alone
Request ground staff approval
Escort or follow the person to the flight deck
Wait until the aircraft is airborne
correct_text: Escort or follow the person to the flight deck

question: When must the flight deck door be closed and locked?
options: At pushback only
Only during taxi and take-off
From the time all external doors are closed after embarkation until they are opened for disembarkation
Only when passengers are seated
correct_text: From the time all external doors are closed after embarkation until they are opened for disembarkation

question: What must be done on aircraft with cargo between the flight deck and cabin?
options: Keep the door open for ventilation
Lock only if turbulence is expected
Lock the door between FWD cargo and cabin during embarkation and flight
Secure with a catering trolley
correct_text: Lock the door between FWD cargo and cabin during embarkation and flight

question: Why must the flight deck door remain locked during flight?
options: To avoid distracting passengers
To ensure flight deck security and prevent unauthorized access
To reduce noise in the cockpit
To allow the crew to rest undisturbed
correct_text: To ensure flight deck security and prevent unauthorized access

question: What are the two flight deck access request modes?
options: Normal and alternate
Scheduled and unscheduled
Normal flight deck access procedure and emergency flight deck access procedure
Manual and automatic
correct_text: Normal flight deck access procedure and emergency flight deck access procedure

question: On aircraft without electromagnetic door locking systems:
options: Door access must be left open for safety
Only ground crew can operate the locks
Locking may be manual but must still be secured during flight
The Commander is not responsible for locking
correct_text: Locking may be manual but must still be secured during flight

question: When does the flight deck become sterile?
options: When boarding starts
After landing
After “Cabin Clear” is given and until seat belt signs are switched OFF
Only during cruise
correct_text: After “Cabin Clear” is given and until seat belt signs are switched OFF

question: During taxi-out after “Cabin Clear” has been given, a passenger reports smoke coming from an overhead bin. What is your BEST action?
options: Wait until take-off is complete before informing SCCM
Notify SCCM and wait for them to decide
Call the flight deck immediately via interphone, even though the flight deck is sterile
Ask other passengers to check and confirm before reporting
correct_text: Call the flight deck immediately via interphone, even though the flight deck is sterile

question: During approach, a CCM notices fluid leaking from an overhead panel near the galley. What is the correct response considering sterile cockpit procedures?
options: Wait until after landing when seatbelt signs are off
Attempt to clean the leak without informing anyone
Report immediately to flight deck via interphone because it could be a safety issue
Make a PA announcement asking passengers to avoid the area
correct_text: Report immediately to flight deck via interphone because it could be a safety issue

question: Which of the following situations justifies breaking sterile flight deck protocol?
options: A passenger requests to change seats
Galley oven stops working
Catering trolley brake feels stiff
Fire, smoke, unusual smells, or abnormal aircraft noises detected
correct_text: Fire, smoke, unusual smells, or abnormal aircraft noises detected

question: Which communication chain is correct during normal (non-emergency) operations?
options: Any CCM calls flight deck directly for every issue
Ground crew relays messages to flight deck
CCM reports to SCCM, SCCM communicates with flight crew
SCCM only communicates after landing
correct_text: CCM reports to SCCM, SCCM communicates with flight crew

question: Cabin crew must ensure passengers are seated so that:
options: Families sit together
All exit rows are occupied first
They will not hinder evacuation and can assist if needed
They match the seat numbers on their boarding passes
correct_text: They will not hinder evacuation and can assist if needed

question: Why must cabin crew avoid placing multiple physically large passengers in the same row segment?
options: To maintain passenger comfort
To prevent overloading the seat structure and ensure quick evacuation access
To allow crew to reach them for meal service
To maintain aircraft center of gravity
correct_text: To prevent overloading the seat structure and ensure quick evacuation access

question: Why must hand baggage be stowed only in approved locations?
options: To maximize overhead space for crew baggage
To keep the cabin looking tidy
To ensure it can be properly restrained and not obstruct passenger movement
To make boarding faster
correct_text: To ensure it can be properly restrained and not obstruct passenger movement

question: What is the correct procedure regarding weight limitations for stowage areas?
options: Ignore weight placards if baggage fits physically
Allow passengers to decide where to place heavy bags
Ensure placarded weight limitations are never exceeded
Distribute heavy items evenly in passenger seats
correct_text: Ensure placarded weight limitations are never exceeded

question: Under-seat stowage may be used only when:
options: The seat is empty
The seat is equipped with a restraint bar and the baggage can be secured
The bag is soft and flexible
Passenger prefers it for easy access
correct_text: The seat is equipped with a restraint bar and the baggage can be secured

question: What is the primary reason cabin crew must check baggage before take-off, landing, and when seatbelt signs are on?
options: To prevent damage to passenger belongings
To make space for crew coats
To ensure baggage will not obstruct evacuation or cause injury by falling
To speed up cabin service
correct_text: To ensure baggage will not obstruct evacuation or cause injury by falling

question: Which of the following is NOT permitted?
options: Placing baggage in overhead lockers that close securely
Stowing coats and jackets in designated crew wardrobe
Placing baggage where it blocks access to emergency equipment or exit handles
Checking baggage security when seat belt sign is on
correct_text: Placing baggage where it blocks access to emergency equipment or exit handles

question: When cabin space is insufficient for a passenger’s hand baggage, what must cabin crew do?
options: Ask passenger to keep it on their lap
Place it on a vacant seat and secure with seat belt
Tag the baggage as DAA and have it stowed in the cargo compartment
Leave it in the galley area until after take-off
correct_text: Tag the baggage as DAA and have it stowed in the cargo compartment

question: Before accepting a DAA bag for loading into the cargo compartment, cabin crew must ensure it does NOT contain:
options: Duty-free shopping items
Medication, documentation, valuables, or carry-on–only dangerous goods such as lithium batteries
Clothing and shoes
correct_text: Medication, documentation, valuables, or carry-on–only dangerous goods such as lithium batteries

question: What is the rule regarding hand baggage in lavatories?
options: Allowed if passenger requests
Not allowed unless lavatory is placarded and weight limitation is observed
Allowed only during cruise
Allowed for crew members only
correct_text: Not allowed unless lavatory is placarded and weight limitation is observed

question: What is CBBG (Cabin Baggage in Seat)?
options: Any baggage placed in overhead compartments
Passenger’s carry-on placed under the seat
Baggage for which a separate seat is purchased, such as valuable or fragile items
Baggage placed in crew storage
correct_text: Baggage for which a separate seat is purchased, such as valuable or fragile items

question: Which of the following is NOT a permitted characteristic of properly secured CBBG?
options: Secured by a seatbelt or restraint strong enough to prevent shifting
Packaged to avoid injury to passengers or crew
Positioned to allow full access to emergency exits and aisles
Placed next to a bulkhead seat
correct_text: Placed next to a bulkhead seat

question: Where should CBBG be positioned relative to the aisle?
options: In the aisle seat for quick removal
In the middle seat if possible
In the seat furthest from the aisle
Next to the emergency exit
correct_text: In the seat furthest from the aisle

question: Cabin crew must verify that CBBG:
options: Is light enough to lift with one hand
Does not exceed seat load limitations and is secured to prevent movement
Is placed near an exit for easier unloading
Is kept unrestrained during cruise for passenger access
correct_text: Does not exceed seat load limitations and is secured to prevent movement

question: Why must CBBG not obstruct view of seatbelt, no smoking, or exit signs?
options: To maintain cabin appearance
To allow passengers to photograph the cabin
So passengers can comply with visual safety instructions
To allow crew to conduct cabin service more efficiently
correct_text: So passengers can comply with visual safety instructions

question: Passengers traveling with children should be seated:
options: In separate rows for weight distribution
Away from exits
Next to or near each other, ideally within one row or aisle
At opposite ends of the cabin
correct_text: Next to or near each other, ideally within one row or aisle

question: When moving passengers for aircraft balance control, cabin crew should:
options: Move passengers freely to any available seat
Move passengers to exit rows for better weight distribution
Move passengers within their section only, unless flight crew permission is obtained
Always move passengers toward the front of the aircraft
correct_text: Move passengers within their section only, unless flight crew permission is obtained

question: Why must at least one passenger be seated on each side of a row with direct access to an emergency exit during taxi, take-off, and landing?
options: To make boarding faster
To balance the cabin visually
To ensure someone is present to operate the exit if needed
To reserve the space for crew baggage
correct_text: To ensure someone is present to operate the exit if needed

question: During boarding, the flight is only 40% full. You notice both seats next to an overwing exit are empty. What is the MOST appropriate action?
options: Leave the seats empty to allow easier crew access.
Fill the seats with any passengers to balance the cabin.
Seat at least one able-bodied passenger on each side of the exit row, unless doing so would violate mass and balance limitations
Assign those seats to parents with infants for extra space.
correct_text: Seat at least one able-bodied passenger on each side of the exit row, unless doing so would violate mass and balance limitations

question: A healthy passenger seated at an exit row is wearing noise-cancelling headphones and refuses to remove them during the safety briefing. What is the BEST response?
options: Allow them to stay since they are physically fit.
Reseat them in a non-exit row and assign someone willing and attentive to assist
Repeat the briefing at a louder volume and let them remain.
Allow them to stay if they promise to remove the headphones in an actual emergency.
correct_text: Reseat them in a non-exit row and assign someone willing and attentive to assist

question: Taxi out, faint smell of smoke in cabin. An anxious exit-row passenger reaches for the handle. Which briefing element—if delivered properly—MOST directly prevents this unsafe action?
options: Taxi out, faint smell of smoke in cabin. An anxious exit-row passenger reaches for the handle. Which briefing element—if delivered properly—MOST directly prevents this unsafe action?
“Do not open the emergency exit unless instructed by the crew.”
“Call other passengers toward the exit once opened.”
correct_text: “Do not open the emergency exit unless instructed by the crew.”

question: You’re briefing a left overwing (Type III) exit. Which instruction BEST captures the hazard-check decision before operating the exit?
options: “Open immediately; the slide will handle any fire.”
“If you see any hazard outside, wait for the captain’s PA.”
“Before opening, look outside. If there’s fire/debris/obstruction at this exit, do NOT open—redirect people to the nearest usable exit.”
“Open first, then decide whether to redirect passengers.”
correct_text: “Before opening, look outside. If there’s fire/debris/obstruction at this exit, do NOT open—redirect people to the nearest usable exit.”

question: During the briefing, one exit-row passenger nods but seems distracted. What is the BEST way to establish understanding and compliance?
options: Accept the nod; time is short.
Hand them the safety card only.
Ask them to verbally confirm willingness and briefly repeat back key steps; if unsure/unwilling, reseat and brief a replacement.
Promise to re-brief after take-off
correct_text: Ask them to verbally confirm willingness and briefly repeat back key steps; if unsure/unwilling, reseat and brief a replacement.

question: You have a BLIND passenger traveling alone seated in row 15. What is the MOST appropriate action before take-off?
options: Provide no additional briefing, as crew safety demo is enough.
Offer a tactile safety briefing, explain location of exits, and confirm they understand evacuation procedures.
Move them automatically to an exit row for easier evacuation.
Provide them a seat belt extension for comfort, regardless of size.
correct_text: Offer a tactile safety briefing, explain location of exits, and confirm they understand evacuation procedures.

question: Which passenger MUST be seated with an escort or companion?
options: WCHR passenger
MAAS passenger
DEAF/BLIND passenger who cannot move unaided
INF (infant under 2 years old)
correct_text: DEAF/BLIND passenger who cannot move unaided

question: You are informed of a DEPU (deportee unescorted) traveling in economy class. What is your primary safety consideration?
options: Give them priority boarding.
Provide a seat near an emergency exit.
Seat them away from exits, brief cabin crew discreetly, and follow security procedures
Place them next to the flight deck door for supervision.
correct_text: Seat them away from exits, brief cabin crew discreetly, and follow security procedures

question: A passenger at 33 weeks pregnant with one baby arrives for check-in with no medical certificate. What is the correct action?
options: Deny boarding immediately.
Require medical clearance before boarding.
Accept her without a medical certificate, as she is within the unrestricted period (≤34 weeks).
Seat her in an exit row for comfort.
correct_text: Accept her without a medical certificate, as she is within the unrestricted period (≤34 weeks).

question: A passenger carrying twins presents a valid medical certificate dated 15 days ago and is at 30 weeks pregnancy. What should you do?
options: Accept without issue; certificate validity is not critical.
Deny boarding as she exceeds DAT’s policy.
Request updated documentation because the certificate is valid for only 14 days from date of issue.
Allow boarding only if the flight time is under 4 hours.
correct_text: Request updated documentation because the certificate is valid for only 14 days from date of issue.

question: At what point is DAT policy to refuse carriage for a pregnant passenger carrying one baby?
options: 34 weeks and beyond
36 weeks and beyond
38 weeks and beyond
40 weeks and beyond
correct_text: 38 weeks and beyond

question: Which seating restriction applies to all expectant mothers?
options: Must be seated at the rear for quicker disembarkation
Must not be seated in an emergency exit row or first row
Must be seated in aisle seat only
Must be seated next to crew jump seat for monitoring
correct_text: Must not be seated in an emergency exit row or first row

question: During cabin checks, you notice an expectant mother’s lap belt positioned across the belly. What is the correct action?
options: Do nothing, as it is her choice.
Ask her to loosen the belt for comfort.
Brief her to reposition the seat belt low, under the belly, for safety
Offer her a belt extension so the belt crosses higher.
correct_text: Brief her to reposition the seat belt low, under the belly, for safety

question: A passenger traveling alone arrives with two infants. What is the MOST compliant solution?
options: Allow one infant to sit on the lap and the second to share the lap during cruise.
Deny boarding for both infants.
Require either a second adult companion (≥16 years) or book an extra seat with an approved Child Restraint Device (CRD) for one infant.
Seat them together in an exit row for easier evacuation.
correct_text: Require either a second adult companion (≥16 years) or book an extra seat with an approved Child Restraint Device (CRD) for one infant.

question: During boarding, you notice a passenger with an infant seated in the first row. What is the correct action?
options: Leave them there for extra legroom.
Move them closer to the exit for quicker evacuation.
Reseat them away from first row as infants must not be seated there.
Place them in a jump seat during take-off.
correct_text: Reseat them away from first row as infants must not be seated there.

question: What is the primary limitation regarding the number of infants per row segment on Airbus aircraft?
options: Number of seats available.
Cabin crew preference.
Number of oxygen masks installed per row segment must not be exceeded.
Number of available infant belts.
correct_text: Number of oxygen masks installed per row segment must not be exceeded.

question: A 10-year-old arrives at the gate, traveling alone but not marked as a UM on the Passenger Information Sheet. What is the MOST appropriate action?
options: Board them as a regular passenger since they are over 5 years old.
Ask another passenger to act as their guardian during the flight.
Inform Ground Operations that the passenger must be processed as a UM before boarding and reflected on the Passenger Information Sheet
Allow them to sit near an exit for extra legroom.
correct_text: Inform Ground Operations that the passenger must be processed as a UM before boarding and reflected on the Passenger Information Sheet

question: When seating UMs, which of the following is correct?
options: They may sit at an over-wing exit if an able-bodied adult is next to them.
They should always be seated in the very last row.
They must not be seated in exit rows or first row and should be placed near cabin crew for supervision (typically FWD or AFT seats.Window seats.
They should be seated next to a passenger with an infant for additional care.
correct_text: They must not be seated in exit rows or first row and should be placed near cabin crew for supervision (typically FWD or AFT seats.Window seats.

question: A UM seated in 14A is next to an adult passenger. What specific instructions should you give that adult?
options: Only to monitor the UM quietly.
To assist the UM in fitting an oxygen mask, securing/releasing seatbelt, and calling crew in any emergency
To keep the UM entertained during flight.
To escort the UM off the aircraft after landing.
correct_text: To assist the UM in fitting an oxygen mask, securing/releasing seatbelt, and calling crew in any emergency

question: You have four PRMs on today’s flight. What is your responsibility before departure?
options: Ensure they are seated anywhere they prefer without reporting it.
Load them last to avoid delaying other passengers.
Notify the Commander of the final number of PRMs on board and ensure they are appropriately briefed and seated
Seat all PRMs near the overwing exits for quick evacuation.
correct_text: Notify the Commander of the final number of PRMs on board and ensure they are appropriately briefed and seated

question: During the personal briefing of a PRM with a mobility aid (e.g., wheelchair), what must you inform them?
options: To keep their mobility aid with them in the cabin for safety.
That the mobility aid must be left behind in an emergency evacuation
That crew will retrieve their mobility aid before evacuation.
That they can only evacuate after all able-bodied passengers.
correct_text: That the mobility aid must be left behind in an emergency evacuation

question: A passenger with lower limb disability is traveling alone. Which seat allocation is MOST appropriat
options: First row seat near galley for service.
Exit row seat for quick egress.
Seat near a floor-level exit with unobstructed access
Middle seat in a full row for stability.
correct_text: Seat near a floor-level exit with unobstructed access

question: A passenger with upper limb disability is traveling without an accompanying person. What is your primary seating consideration?
options: Seat them near lavatories for convenience.
Seat them near overwing exits to assist with balance.
Allocate a seat that allows visual and audible communication with cabin crew
Seat them in a middle seat to ensure other passengers can assist.
correct_text: Allocate a seat that allows visual and audible communication with cabin crew

question: In a decompression scenario, which is the correct sequence for an accompanying passenger assisting an SCP (Severely Disabled Passenger)?
options: Fit the SCP’s oxygen mask first, then their own.
Wait for cabin crew to fit both masks.
Fit their own mask first, then assist the SCP with theirs
Do nothing unless instructed by crew.
correct_text: Fit their own mask first, then assist the SCP with theirs

question: During boarding, you are approached by a blind passenger using a cane who declines assistance. What is your BEST action?
options: Escort them firmly to their seat regardless.
Assign them a special boarding priority.
Respect their independence but offer assistance if needed, introducing yourself by light touch on the shoulder and staying half a step ahead if they accept
Refuse boarding until an escort is provided.
correct_text: Respect their independence but offer assistance if needed, introducing yourself by light touch on the shoulder and staying half a step ahead if they accept

question: Which seating-related instruction is MOST helpful during a blind passenger briefing?
options: Describe the color of the seat covers.
Show them the entertainment system controls.
Explain seatbelt operation, number of rows to nearest exit, and location of call button, lavatory, and oxygen masks
Tell them only that crew will assist in case of emergency.
correct_text: Explain seatbelt operation, number of rows to nearest exit, and location of call button, lavatory, and oxygen masks

question: A deaf passenger cannot lip read. Which is the BEST way to brief them on safety procedures?
options: Write the instructions down or use visual aids (safety card/demonstration equipment)
Allow them to observe other passengers and assume compliance.
Skip the individual briefing since they are seated.
Shout and repeat the instructions multiple times.
correct_text: Write the instructions down or use visual aids (safety card/demonstration equipment)

question: During a decompression event, how should you ensure a deaf passenger follows oxygen mask procedures?
options: Rely on PA announcements only.
Write instructions on paper before donning your mask.
Wait for the passenger to notice crew actions.
Demonstrate visually and point clearly to mask while securing your own first
correct_text: Demonstrate visually and point clearly to mask while securing your own first

question: During boarding, you encounter a passenger who appears agitated, shouting, and refusing to follow crew instructions. What is the correct procedure?
options: Allow them to board to avoid delaying the flight.
Consult with the Commander — carriage may be refused if the passenger poses a flight safety risk
Provide extra seating space and let them fly unaccompanied.
Ask another passenger to supervise them.
correct_text: Consult with the Commander — carriage may be refused if the passenger poses a flight safety risk

question: Under what condition may a mentally impaired passenger travel unaccompanied?
options: When seated away from other passengers.
When ground staff confirms they are calm at check-in.
When they can understand and follow flight safety and security instructions
When they have a written statement from their family.
correct_text: When they can understand and follow flight safety and security instructions

question: When must a mentally impaired passenger be accompanied by a guardian or nurse?
options: Always, regardless of their ability.
When they cannot remain calm or might not follow crew instructions, posing a safety risk
Only on flights longer than four hours.
Only on international flights.
correct_text: When they cannot remain calm or might not follow crew instructions, posing a safety risk

question: Why might a passenger with a Sunflower lanyard need additional time or assistance?
options: They may be carrying excess baggage.
They are entitled to priority service by law.
They may have a non-visible condition (e.g., autism, sensory impairment, chronic pain) that affects how they process tasks
They require cabin crew to sit next to them during the flight.
correct_text: They may have a non-visible condition (e.g., autism, sensory impairment, chronic pain) that affects how they process tasks

question: A passenger boards with a privately owned POB and no prior approval from DAT. What is the correct procedure?
options: Allow boarding since it is for medical purposes.
Accept only if the bottle looks clean and undamaged.
Deny carriage until proper DAT approval and medical clearance are confirmed
Store the bottle in the galley for safety.
correct_text: Deny carriage until proper DAT approval and medical clearance are confirmed

question: When must the SCCM inform the Commander about passengers with POB?
options: Only if more than 3 bottles are on board.
Only if passengers are seated in exit rows.
Before departure, providing verbal information about the number of POBs and their locations
After take-off during cabin secure check.
correct_text: Before departure, providing verbal information about the number of POBs and their locations

question: Which of the following requirements is NOT acceptable for POB carriage?
options: POB manufactured for medical use.
Weight of POB ≤ 5 kg, containing no liquid oxygen.
Valves and regulators protected from damage.
POB with visible cracks but partially functional
correct_text: POB with visible cracks but partially functional

question: Where should a passenger with a POB ideally be seated?
options: In an emergency exit row for quick evacuation.
In an aisle seat for easier access.
In a window seat if possible, away from emergency exits
Near the galley for crew supervision.
correct_text: In a window seat if possible, away from emergency exits

question: During boarding, you notice a POB is loosely placed on the passenger’s lap. What is the correct action?
options: Leave it as is, as long as it is turned off.
Instruct the passenger to secure it in their own approved stowage so it remains secured during all phases of flight
Place it under a spare seat for them.
Ask them to hold it during taxi, take-off, and landing for accessibility.
correct_text: Instruct the passenger to secure it in their own approved stowage so it remains secured during all phases of flight

question: A passenger boards with a POC but no medical clearance. What is the correct action?
options: Allow them to fly as long as the POC appears functional.
Request that they provide prior medical clearance confirming both fitness to fly and need for supplemental oxygen
Ask them to switch off the POC during taxi and take-off.
Only require clearance for international flights.
correct_text: Request that they provide prior medical clearance confirming both fitness to fly and need for supplemental oxygen

question: Why are POCs considered Portable Electronic Devices (PEDs)?
options: They are stored in overhead lockers with other electronics.
They contain lithium batteries and must follow PED safety/fire procedures
They must be switched off during critical phases.
They function as Wi-Fi devices.
correct_text: They contain lithium batteries and must follow PED safety/fire procedures

question: In case of smoke or fire involving a POC, what procedure should crew follow?
options: Ask the passenger to unplug the device and continue use after cooling.
Follow lithium battery fire procedures, using appropriate firefighting and containmen
Move the device to an empty seat away from passengers.
Place the device in a lavatory and close the door.
correct_text: Follow lithium battery fire procedures, using appropriate firefighting and containmen

question: Which seating and stowage requirements apply to passengers with POCs?
options: POC must be placed on lap for quick access and passenger seated in exit row.
POC may be left unsecured if flight is short.
POC must fit under the seat, be stowed and secured during all phases, and passenger must NOT be seated in an exit row
POC must always be placed in an overhead locker during take-off and landing.
correct_text: POC must fit under the seat, be stowed and secured during all phases, and passenger must NOT be seated in an exit row

question: During pre-flight briefing, what is a critical point cabin crew must communicate to a passenger using a POC?
options: To remove batteries before flight to avoid interference.
To stop using POC during decompression events.
To use drop-down oxygen masks in case of decompression, despite having a POC
To keep POC turned off during turbulence to save battery.
correct_text: To use drop-down oxygen masks in case of decompression, despite having a POC

question: During boarding, a passenger presents a dog wearing a harness but no documentation, claiming it is a “therapy companion dog.” What is the correct action?
options: Accept the dog as long as it is calm.
Seat the dog anywhere with its owner.
Deny cabin carriage unless it is a bona fide service dog (trained to perform tasks for a disability) or emotional support animal with proper documentation
correct_text: Deny cabin carriage unless it is a bona fide service dog (trained to perform tasks for a disability) or emotional support animal with proper documentation

question: Where must a recognized assistance dog be seated during the flight?
options: In the passenger’s lap for reassurance.
On an empty seat next to the passenger.
On the floor at the passenger’s feet, ideally at a bulkhead window seat to keep aisles clear
In the aisle for quick access to the lavatory.
correct_text: On the floor at the passenger’s feet, ideally at a bulkhead window seat to keep aisles clear

question: What is the correct decompression procedure briefing for a passenger traveling with a service dog?
options: Secure dog first, then put on own mask.
Wait for crew to fit the dog’s mask.
First secure their own oxygen mask, fasten seatbelt, then place spare mask over dog’s muzzle
Leave the dog unsecured until the situation stabilizes.
correct_text: First secure their own oxygen mask, fasten seatbelt, then place spare mask over dog’s muzzle

question: If a nearby passenger complains of allergies to the service dog, what is the correct cabin crew response?
options: Remove the service dog from the cabin.
Ask the owner to move to a different seat.
Re-seat the complaining passenger while keeping the service dog with its owner
Provide a face mask to the passenger and do nothing else.
correct_text: Re-seat the complaining passenger while keeping the service dog with its owner

question: A passenger boards with a cat in a hard plastic crate that is slightly too tall to fit under the seat. What is the correct action?
options: Allow it as long as the crate door is closed securely.
Place the crate in an empty seat next to the passenger.
Deny carriage unless the passenger provides an approved soft carrier that fits under the seat
Allow the crate to remain partially in the aisle during flight.
correct_text: Deny carriage unless the passenger provides an approved soft carrier that fits under the seat

question: What is the maximum allowed combined weight of the PETC and carrier bag?
options: 5 kg
8 kg
10 kg
No weight limit is specified.
correct_text: 8 kg

question: Two small dogs belonging to the same passenger are traveling together. Under what condition can they be accepted in one carrier?
options: If their combined weight does not exceed 10 kg.
If they are puppies under 6 months old.
If they are of the same species and total weight does not exceed 8 kg
If the passenger pays for an additional seat.
correct_text: If they are of the same species and total weight does not exceed 8 kg

question: Which of the following animals are NOT allowed to travel in the passenger cabin?
options: Small dogs under 8 kg
Cats with valid vaccination papers
Rodents
Two puppies of the same litter
correct_text: Rodents

question: During boarding, a passenger complains of allergies to a nearby PETC. What is the correct crew response?
options: Deny travel to the passenger with the PETC.
Ask the passenger with the PETC to put the animal in the galley.
Re-seat the allergic passenger away from the PETC
Allow them to remain seated together and monitor the situation.
correct_text: Re-seat the allergic passenger away from the PETC

question: During final approach, a cabin crew member is seen using their phone to check messages while seated on the jump seat. What is the correct action?
options: Allow it if the phone is in flight mode.
Prohibit PED use — crew must focus on safety-related duties during take-off/landing
Allow as long as no passengers see them.
Permit if the device is used only for entertainment.
correct_text: Prohibit PED use — crew must focus on safety-related duties during take-off/landing

question: Where are PEDs NOT permitted to be stored?
options: In the crew bag
In overhead stowage
In or behind crew seats
In galley compartments
correct_text: In or behind crew seats

question: A crew member uses their tablet to review the cabin crew operating manual during taxi-out. Is this acceptable?
options: No, PED use is prohibited during taxi under all circumstances.
Yes, provided it is operational use, device is secured after use, and does not interfere with safety duties
Yes, as long as passengers are not watching.
No, unless approved by the Commander.
correct_text: Yes, provided it is operational use, device is secured after use, and does not interfere with safety duties

question: During Low Visibility Operations (LVOs), which is the correct PED procedure?
options: Keep PEDs in flight mode.
Use PEDs freely as long as no RF interference occurs.
Completely power off PEDs as per EASA requirements
Switch to airplane Wi-Fi mode only.
correct_text: Completely power off PEDs as per EASA requirements

question: A crew member is found watching a movie on their phone during the cruise. What is the correct response by SCCM?
options: Allow it if the passenger cabin is calm.
Ignore it since PEDs are allowed in flight.
Remind crew that PEDs must not be used for entertainment purposes and may negatively impact passenger perception
Permit as long as the movie has no sound.
correct_text: Remind crew that PEDs must not be used for entertainment purposes and may negatively impact passenger perception

question: Who has the ultimate responsibility for passenger safety during embarkation?
options: Ground Handling Supervisor
SCCM
Commander and DAT representatives
Airport Security
correct_text: Commander and DAT representatives

question: When can Ground Staff begin passenger boarding?
options: As soon as the aircraft arrives on stand.
Only after receiving a clear signal from Cabin Crew
When passengers are gathered at the gate, regardless of crew readiness.
When the SCCM announces boarding over the PA.
correct_text: Only after receiving a clear signal from Cabin Crew

question: When may Cabin Crew begin boarding passengers?
options: As soon as they are ready with pre-flight checks.
After the first passengers arrive from the gate.
Only after receiving clear signals from both Ground Staff and Commander
Immediately after the “boarding in 5 minutes” gate announcement.
correct_text: Only after receiving clear signals from both Ground Staff and Commander

question: While greeting passengers, you notice a passenger who smells strongly of alcohol and is staggering. What is the correct action?
options: Allow them to board quietly to avoid a scene.
Wait until after take-off to assess their behavior.
Refuse embarkation if necessary, but make the final decision together with the Commander
Seat them at the back of the cabin so they disturb fewer passengers.
correct_text: Refuse embarkation if necessary, but make the final decision together with the Commander

question: During boarding, you notice a passenger placing a heavy bag on the floor in the exit row. What should you do?
options: Leave it if the passenger insists.
Instruct the passenger to stow it in the overhead bin
Allow it as long as the bag is small.
Move the bag to another passenger’s seat row.
correct_text: Instruct the passenger to stow it in the overhead bin

question: During boarding, a passenger tries to bring a container with flammable liquid onto the aircraft. What is the correct crew response?
options: Store it safely in the galley.
Allow it if sealed tightly.
Refuse carriage and alert Commander/ground staff as it constitutes dangerous goods
Ask passenger to keep it under the seat and not open it.
correct_text: Refuse carriage and alert Commander/ground staff as it constitutes dangerous goods

question: Which step is necessary before take-off for passengers seated at emergency exits?
options: Offer them free headsets for comfort.
Skip briefing if they look capable.
Provide a verbal briefing on their responsibilities in case of emergency
Re-seat them all in the front rows.
correct_text: Provide a verbal briefing on their responsibilities in case of emergency

question: Who is responsible for giving the “Boarding Complete” signal?
options: Ground staff only
Flight Deck crew only
SCCM or the Commander
Any cabin crew member available
correct_text: SCCM or the Commander

question: What is the purpose of the Seating Report?
options: To record the seat numbers of special category passengers only
To verify crew positions during boarding
To confirm the actual number of passengers on board matches the number checked in
To list all passengers’ meal preferences
correct_text: To confirm the actual number of passengers on board matches the number checked in

question: When is SCCM required to fill out and sign the Seating Report?
options: At the end of the flight
Only if requested by ground handling
If required by the Commander
Only on flights with more than 100 passengers
correct_text: If required by the Commander

question: After completing the Seating Report, SCCM must:
options: Store it in the galley documentation folder
Read it aloud to passengers
Give it to the Commander
Email it to ground operations
correct_text: Give it to the Commander

question: After the seating report is given, who gives the command to close the doors?
options: SCCM automatically after passenger count
Commander
Ground handling staff
Any available cabin crew
correct_text: Commander

question: On an ATR72, what is the correct phrase SCCM/CCM1 must report to the Commander after closing doors?
options: "Doors closed, ready for taxi"
"Doors locked"
"Door(s) closed, tail prop on board"
"Cabin secure"
correct_text: "Door(s) closed, tail prop on board"

question: What is the correct SCCM PA command for door closure on A320/A321?
options: “Cabin Crew, Close doors and wait for confirmation.”
“Cabin Crew, Close doors, prepare cabin.”
“Cabin Crew, Close doors, Arm slides, Cross-Check and Report”
“Cabin Crew, Arm slides, Cabin Secure.”
correct_text: “Cabin Crew, Close doors, Arm slides, Cross-Check and Report”

question: When arming doors, what is the correct sequence described by the phrase “PIN – LEVER – PIN”?
options: Insert pin, check lever, remove pin
Remove pin, close door, reinsert pin
Remove safety pin, select ARMED, insert pin into stowage hole
Pull lever first, then remove and discard safety pin
correct_text: Remove safety pin, select ARMED, insert pin into stowage hole

question: When must the safety demonstration be performed?
options: At cruise altitude
After the first beverage service
As soon as possible after doors have been closed
After the aircraft begins taxiing
correct_text: As soon as possible after doors have been closed

question: What language must be used for the safety demonstration when operating in a foreign country?
options: The native language of the cabin crew
Only the local language of the departure airport
English, and a second language if necessary as per announcement folder
No language requirement, demonstration only
correct_text: English, and a second language if necessary as per announcement folder

question: After receiving “Cabin Clear” from all CCMs, what is SCCM’s next step?
options: Begin inflight announcements
Proceed to check flight deck door
Report “Cabin Clear” via interphone to Commander
correct_text: Report “Cabin Clear” via interphone to Commander

question: During approach, you are performing galley cleanup when the F/D gives the final call for cabin crew to be seated. What is the correct action?
options: Finish cleaning quickly before sitting down.
Secure loose items but remain standing until landing is imminent.
Stop immediately, take assigned jump seat, fasten harness, and begin silent review
Wait for SCCM’s personal instruction before sitting down.
correct_text: Stop immediately, take assigned jump seat, fasten harness, and begin silent review

question: Which of the following is NOT part of the silent review?
options: Operation of exits
Location of emergency equipment
Commands and brace positions
Passenger meal orders
correct_text: Passenger meal orders

question: What is the correct seating position during take-off and landing?
options: Feet crossed, hands folded in lap
Feet flat, one hand on interphone
Feet flat on the floor, back straight, palms on lap or under thighs
Any comfortable position as long as seatbelt is fastened
correct_text: Feet flat on the floor, back straight, palms on lap or under thighs

question: During silent review, you hear an unusual grinding noise and smell smoke. What is your immediate action?
options: Wait until after take-off to inform the flight crew.
Alert the nearest passenger to prepare for evacuation.
Immediately contact the flight deck via interphone to report the abnormal situation
Assume it is normal engine noise and remain silent.
correct_text: Immediately contact the flight deck via interphone to report the abnormal situation

question: Why is silent review important during take-off and landing?
options: It helps cabin crew stay calm and think about service sequence.
It gives crew time to check personal devices before taxi.
It mentally prepares crew for unexpected emergencies and allows faster, more coordinated response
It is only a company policy, not a safety requirement.
correct_text: It mentally prepares crew for unexpected emergencies and allows faster, more coordinated response

question: When must cabin crew remain seated after take-off?
options: Until 10,000 ft altitude is reached
Until the “Fasten Seat Belt” sign has been switched OFF
Until SCCM announces start of service
Until passengers have stowed their bags
correct_text: Until the “Fasten Seat Belt” sign has been switched OFF

question: If no call from the flight deck is received within 30 minutes after take-off, what must SCCM/CCM1 do?
options: Wait until landing to report it
Knock on flight deck door repeatedly
Initiate an interphone call to the flight deck, then repeat contact every 30 minutes
correct_text: Initiate an interphone call to the flight deck, then repeat contact every 30 minutes

question: During the first post-takeoff call, what is the main purpose of establishing two-way communication with the flight deck?
options: To discuss flight service preferences
To confirm flight time to destination
To verify crew well-being and detect early signs of incapacitation
To request weather updates for PA
correct_text: To verify crew well-being and detect early signs of incapacitation

question: During in-flight service, a CCM needs to step away from the cart briefly to assist a passenger. What must they do first?
options: Leave the cart where it is and hurry back
Engage the braking device to prevent cart movement
Ask a nearby passenger to hold the cart
Move the cart to the galley before leaving
correct_text: Engage the braking device to prevent cart movement

question: A passenger shows signs of intoxication but requests another alcoholic drink. What is the correct response?
options: Serve a smaller amount to avoid confrontation
Politely refuse service of alcohol and monitor passenger behavior
Serve them one more drink, then inform SCCM
Offer a complimentary drink to calm them
correct_text: Politely refuse service of alcohol and monitor passenger behavior

question: Before departure, you notice that one catering trolley seal does not match the delivery note. What is your correct action?
options: Ignore it if the trolley appears full.
Wait until after landing to report it.
Inform the Commander immediately and inspect the trolley by breaking/opening the seal
Serve from the trolley and report after service.
correct_text: Inform the Commander immediately and inspect the trolley by breaking/opening the seal

question: Why must flight crew members occupying pilot seats never have the same meal?
options: To avoid passenger complaints
To ensure fairness between pilots
To reduce the risk of both pilots suffering from the same potential foodborne illness
Because one meal is reserved for the cabin crew
correct_text: To reduce the risk of both pilots suffering from the same potential foodborne illness

question: When serving beverages to pilots on an A320/A321, what is the safest procedure?
options: Pass drinks from the center pedestal side
Hand drinks directly from the forward jump seat
Serve from behind and on the window side of the pilot seat, using lids on hot drinks
Ask the pilot to take the drink from the galley to avoid spillage
correct_text: Serve from behind and on the window side of the pilot seat, using lids on hot drinks

question: Why must a cabin crew member maintain a safe distance when handing food or drink to the flight deck crew?
options: To show respect and professionalism
To avoid blocking the view of the instruments
To prevent spillage onto critical flight instruments
To keep the interphone area clear
correct_text: To prevent spillage onto critical flight instruments

question: How often must lavatories be checked during flight?
options: Every hour
Only when a passenger reports a problem
At intervals of 30 minutes
Only before take-off and after landing
correct_text: At intervals of 30 minutes

question: How frequently must cabin crew check passenger cabin conditions for smells, noises, temperature, and passenger well-being?
options: Only when SCCM instructs
Every 30 minutes
At crew discretion if cabin seems quiet
Once per flight segment
correct_text: Every 30 minutes

question: When must cargo compartments that are accessible to passengers be checked?
options: Only if smoke alarm activates
At the start and end of the flight
Every 30 minutes to prevent unauthorized access and monitor for smoke/fire
Only during turbulence
correct_text: Every 30 minutes to prevent unauthorized access and monitor for smoke/fire

question: What is the correct in-flight cleaning practice for passenger touch surfaces?
options: Use alcohol-based sprays for faster disinfection
Clean only after meal service
Use disinfectant wipes and avoid alcohol-based sprays to prevent flammable vapors or false smoke detector activation
Perform cleaning only on long-haul flights
correct_text: Use disinfectant wipes and avoid alcohol-based sprays to prevent flammable vapors or false smoke detector activation

question: Who has the authority to refuse boarding to a passenger visibly under the influence of alcohol?
options: SCCM only
Ground staff supervisor
Commander
Any cabin crew member without consultation
correct_text: Commander

question: A passenger produces their own bottle of alcohol and pours a drink during the flight. What is the correct response?
options: Allow it if they are of legal drinking age
Confiscate it permanently
Politely inform the passenger that only alcohol served by cabin crew may be consumed, and notify Commander if needed
Ignore the situation if the passenger is quiet
correct_text: Politely inform the passenger that only alcohol served by cabin crew may be consumed, and notify Commander if needed

question: Under what circumstances can private alcohol be removed from a passenger for safe custody?
options: Only if the passenger is under 18
Only if the passenger is aggressive
At the discretion of the Commander if it poses a safety or order risk
Only on international flights
correct_text: At the discretion of the Commander if it poses a safety or order risk

question: When a passenger becomes ill mid-flight, what is the cabin crew’s FIRST priority?
options: Fill out the Cabin Report immediately
Attend to the passenger, assess symptoms, and provide necessary first aid
Request landing clearance from ATC
Search for medical equipment in the galley
correct_text: Attend to the passenger, assess symptoms, and provide necessary first aid

question: After the Commander switches ON the fasten seat belt sign for landing, what is SCCM’s immediate next step?
options: Check crew jump seats first
Begin inflight sales closure
Make the before-landing passenger announcement
Call the Commander for landing clearance
correct_text: Make the before-landing passenger announcement

question: What must cabin crew do after making the before-landing announcement?
options: Collect last-minute trash from passengers
Finish any galley work quickly
Stop all cabin activities and perform the full cabin check, including lavatories and galley securement
Brief the passengers on arrival procedures
correct_text: Stop all cabin activities and perform the full cabin check, including lavatories and galley securement

question: When is the Cabin Ready button pressed (if applicable on the aircraft)?
options: After taxiing to gate
Once passengers have disembarked
After receiving “Cabin Secured” and “Cabin Clear” from all crew, before landing
Only in case of an emergency landing
correct_text: After receiving “Cabin Secured” and “Cabin Clear” from all crew, before landing

question: What is considered acceptable when waste bags are temporarily stowed in a lavatory before landing?
options: Waste can be stowed anywhere if lavatory door is locked
Waste must be in a fireproof bag and lavatory placarded
Waste can remain in lavatory without any labeling
correct_text: Waste must be in a fireproof bag and lavatory placarded

question: When should SCCM/CCM make the “After Landing” announcement?
options: Immediately after touchdown
After the seat belt signs are switched off
When the aircraft is off the runway
Only after engines are shut down
correct_text: When the aircraft is off the runway

question: When are cabin crew permitted to leave their seats after landing?
options: Once the aircraft slows below taxi speed
Immediately after aircraft turns off runway
Only after seat belt signs are turned OFF and to perform safety duties
When passengers begin standing
correct_text: Only after seat belt signs are turned OFF and to perform safety duties

question: What must SCCM/CCM1 do if no door-opening signal is received within 3 minutes after Commander’s permission?
options: Wait longer until ground staff arrives
Contact the flight deck to confirm door opening clearance
Open the door carefully without confirmation
Ask passengers to wait at their seats
correct_text: Contact the flight deck to confirm door opening clearance

question: Before letting passengers out on ATR72, what must be confirmed?
options: Catering supplies have been removed
Seat belt signs are still on
Tail prop is installed and propellers have stopped turning
Commander has left the flight deck
correct_text: Tail prop is installed and propellers have stopped turning

question: What is the correct first step before opening a door on A320/A321 in normal operations?
options: Open the door slowly while checking outside
Verify passengers are ready for disembarkation
Stop all other activities, visually check that slide arming lever is DISARMED and safety pin is installed
Unlock the door and open immediately
correct_text: Stop all other activities, visually check that slide arming lever is DISARMED and safety pin is installed

question: Where is the designated cleaning equipment stored if external cleaning is unavailable or insufficient?
options: Forward galley storage compartment
Under passenger seats in the first row
In the aft galley marked with a “Cleaning Items” tag
In the cockpit storage locker
correct_text: In the aft galley marked with a “Cleaning Items” tag

question: What is SCCM’s responsibility if additional time is required for post-flight cleaning duties?
options: Finish as quickly as possible and log time later
Contact ground handling directly for delay approval
Inform the Commander, who passes the information to OCC for check-out adjustments
Notify passengers about the delay
correct_text: Inform the Commander, who passes the information to OCC for check-out adjustments

question: Which of the following is NOT part of the cabin cleaning checklist for next-flight readiness?
options: Remove/replace used catering
Perform a full aircraft security search
Check lavatories and restock supplies
Straighten seat belts
correct_text: Perform a full aircraft security search

question: Who is responsible for initiating a debriefing after each flight?
options: The Commander
SCCM
Any Ground Staff representative
Only Crew Scheduling
correct_text: SCCM

question: What is one of the main purposes of a post-flight debriefing?
options: Collect feedback for catering providers
Distribute crew pay reports
Discuss incidents, share feedback, and improve team performance
Review passenger satisfaction surveys
correct_text: Discuss incidents, share feedback, and improve team performance

question: Who can initiate a debriefing if they feel it is necessary?
options: Only SCCM
Only the most senior Cabin Crew
Any Cabin Crew Member
Only Operations Control
correct_text: Any Cabin Crew Member

question: Why is positive feedback emphasized during debriefings?
options: To avoid reporting negative incidents
To reduce the amount of paperwork
To motivate the crew and reinforce good practices
To avoid delays for the next flight
correct_text: To motivate the crew and reinforce good practices

question: If cleaning onboard is not available during a domestic turnaround, what are crew required to do?
options: Wait until ground staff arrives to clean
Collect trash, arrange seat belts in V position, check and restock lavatories if necessary
Close overhead bins and leave cabin as is
Delay boarding until full cleaning is performed
correct_text: Collect trash, arrange seat belts in V position, check and restock lavatories if necessary

question: What is strictly forbidden on passenger bridges/stairs due to safety and security reasons?
options: Stowing cleaning equipment
Allowing passengers to pass during cleaning
Leaving waste or items from the aircraft
correct_text: Leaving waste or items from the aircraft

question: Which type of cleaning is performed after short flights, before departure, by cabin crew?
options: Full cleaning
Light cleaning
Preventive cleaning
correct_text: Light cleaning

question: What is the purpose of preventive cleaning and disinfection of the aircraft?
options: To speed up boarding times
To comply with local airport rules
To meet EASA safety directives or other regulatory requirements
To prepare aircraft for deep cleaning
correct_text: To meet EASA safety directives or other regulatory requirements

question: Why must disembarking passengers be required to take their cabin baggage with them during a transit stop?
options: To make overhead bins easier to clean
To speed up boarding for new passengers
To prevent unattended baggage from being left behind and to meet security requirements
To allow ground staff to disinfect bins
correct_text: To prevent unattended baggage from being left behind and to meet security requirements

question: What is the correct procedure for reconciling remaining passengers during a transit stop?
options: Only count passengers seated in front rows
Check boarding passes without moving passengers
Count passengers, ask them to identify their cabin baggage, place it on their lap/seat, and search overhead bins
Ask only UM, PRM, and VIP passengers to present baggage
correct_text: Count passengers, ask them to identify their cabin baggage, place it on their lap/seat, and search overhead bins

question: Under what conditions can a security search be conducted while passengers remain on board during transit stop
options: When there is no time for a full search
When the aircraft is parked at a remote stand
When passengers have their baggage with them and are under supervision to prevent movement
When only half the passengers have disembarked
correct_text: When passengers have their baggage with them and are under supervision to prevent movement

question: What must the cabin crew do after completing the security search at a transit stop?
options: Immediately begin boarding new passengers
Wait for ground staff to sign off the manifest
Inform the Commander of the search results
Record findings in the Catering Delivery Notes
correct_text: Inform the Commander of the search results

question: What is the correct cabin configuration during refueling?
options: Cabin lights dimmed, curtains closed, doors armed
Cabin lights on, emergency lights off, doors closed
Cabin lights on, curtains open and secured, emergency lights ON, aisles and exits clear
Curtains closed for passenger privacy and crew seated
correct_text: Cabin lights on, curtains open and secured, emergency lights ON, aisles and exits clear

question: What is the correct action if fuel vapors are detected inside the cabin?
options: Continue fueling but monitor situation
Open all overhead bins to ventilate cabin
Immediately inform Commander, who will stop fueling and may order passenger disembarkation
Close all doors and keep passengers seated until smell is gone
correct_text: Immediately inform Commander, who will stop fueling and may order passenger disembarkation

question: What does the Commander announce to initiate a non-emergency but rapid passenger offload during refueling hazard or other abnormal situation?
options: “Cabin Crew, Prepare for Evacuation”
“Cabin Crew, Open All Doors”
“QUICK DISEMBARKATION”
“Cabin Crew, Standby for Evacuation”
correct_text: “QUICK DISEMBARKATION”
